title: Roz bi laban
subtitle: Egyptian rice pudding with rose water
excerpt: Short-grain rice cooked low and slow in whole milk until the grains are dissolved into a thick, creamy pudding, then scented with rose water and vanilla. Served cold in small dishes, dusted with cinnamon. The Egyptian national dessert, sold in glass dishes at every street food stall and restaurant.
sourceNotes: Roz bi laban (rice in milk) is the fundamental Egyptian dessert, eaten throughout the Arab world under various names (roz bel laban, riz bi halib). The Egyptian version is thick and creamy, served cold, and scented with rose water. It has been a staple of Egyptian street food and home cooking for centuries.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Must be made ahead — needs at least 2 hours of chilling.

content.text: Use short-grain or pudding rice: the high starch content is what makes the pudding thick and creamy as the grains dissolve. Long-grain rice will not produce the same result. Cook over the lowest possible heat, stirring every few minutes — the milk scorches easily.

The rose water is added off the heat, not during cooking: cooking volatilises the fragrant compounds and you lose the effect. Use it sparingly — 1 tablespoon is enough for a subtle floral note. More than 2 tablespoons and the pudding tastes of soap.

Combine the rinsed {{short-grain-rice}}, {{whole-milk}}, {{granulated-sugar}}, {{vanilla-extract}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a heavy saucepan. Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat, stirring. Reduce to the lowest possible heat and cook for 35 to 40 minutes, stirring every 3 to 4 minutes, until the rice is very soft and the pudding is thick and creamy. The rice should almost dissolve into the milk — scrape the bottom of the pan each time you stir to prevent scorching. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{rose-water}}. Pour into individual serving dishes or a large bowl. Leave to cool for 20 minutes, then refrigerate for at least 2 hours. Dust with {{cinnamon-ground}} before serving.

items.cause: Not cooked down enough or too much milk
items.fix: The pudding thickens significantly as it chills — it should be almost pourable when hot but set to a thick consistency when cold. Continue simmering if it looks very liquid when hot

items.cause: Heat too high or not stirred frequently enough
items.fix: Use the lowest possible heat setting. Stir every 3 minutes without exception. A heavy-bottomed saucepan distributes heat more evenly than a thin pan

items.cause: Too much added or poor quality rose water
items.fix: Use 0.5 tablespoon for a subtle note. Some rose waters are much stronger than others — taste a drop and adjust. Always add off the heat

content.text: Roz bi laban is Egypt's dessert. It appears at the end of every substantial meal, is sold from carts on the street, and is made at home for feeding children and recovering from illness. In Egyptian tradition it is the dessert of welcome and comfort. The same dish is made across the Arab world (Lebanon, Syria, Iraq), each with slight regional variations in sweetness, creaminess, and flavouring. The Egyptian version is characteristically thick and lightly rose-scented, served cold in a small glass dish with a dusting of cinnamon.
